learning.summary: Open-source software makes its source code available for inspection, modification, and redistribution under its license. It is commonly developed and maintained by a community of contributors.
learning.explanation:
Open-source software allows users and developers to view and improve the program's source code. This collaborative development model often results in contributions from individuals and organizations around the world.

Unlike closed-source software, where the source code is proprietary and controlled by its owner, open-source software encourages transparency and community participation. Linux is a well-known example of an open-source operating system family.

The availability of source code does not necessarily mean software is free of cost or without licensing requirements; usage is governed by the software's license.

facts.text: Open-source software provides access to its source code.
Open-source projects are often maintained by a community of contributors.
Closed-source software does not make its source code publicly available.
Linux is a common example of open-source software.

examples.text: An organization deploys a Linux distribution because it prefers an open-source operating system supported by a large community.

examTips: Know the difference between open-source and closed-source software.
commonMistakes: Assuming open source always means free of charge or unsupported.
scenarios: Choose between open-source and closed-source software for a deployment.
pbqIdeas: Classify software examples as open source or closed source.

reviewNotes: Added clarification that licensing terms still apply to open-source software as contextual enrichment beyond the transcript evidence.
